topic: The Disc Diameter: 40,030 km and Its Provenance
our_argument_summary: Section 6.2 argues that the dome's disc diameter of 40,030 km is derived from the globe's circumference, not independently discovered. It focuses on WIN-062 (Tesla longitudinal wave speed): the dome calculates v = 40,030 / 0.08484 = 471,829 km/s = 1.574c. Our section makes three main points: (1) 40,030 km ≈ 40,075 km (globe equatorial circumference) to 0.11%, so the disc diameter is the globe circumference relabeled; (2) Tesla's own patent US 787412 describes surface wave propagation on a spherical Earth with an actual globe diagram; (3) the ~1.57c superluminal phase velocity is a well-documented Earth-ionosphere waveguide effect that only exists on a sphere.

holes_found.description: Section 6.2 frames the provenance argument as '40,030 km is suspiciously close to 40,075 km — Earth's equatorial circumference.' But 40,030 ≠ 40,075; they're 45 km apart. A dome defender can say 'those aren't the same number — our diameter is 40,030, not 40,075.' The STRONGER argument is already made in Section 2.9: the disc radius 20,015 km = π × R_Earth (6,371 km) to within 114 meters (0.0006%). That means the disc diameter 40,030 km = 2πR_Earth to within 228 meters. This is the mathematically devastating relationship: there is no geometric reason a flat disc's diameter should equal 2π times anything — the factor π enters because 20,015 km is really the pole-to-antipode arc length on a sphere (∫₀^π R dθ = πR). Section 6.2 should USE this argument from 2.9 rather than the weaker 'close to equatorial circumference' framing. The 2.9 argument is airtight; the 6.2 argument has a 45 km gap a defender can exploit.
holes_found.recommendation: Revise the provenance paragraph to lead with the 2πR relationship (40,030 = 2π × 6,371 to 0.0006%) rather than the equatorial circumference comparison (40,030 vs 40,075, 0.11% gap). Cross-reference Section 2.9 explicitly for the πR derivation. The equatorial circumference comparison can remain as a secondary observation, but the πR argument should be primary because it's geometrically explanatory (π appears because the distance is an arc on a sphere) rather than merely numerically suggestive.

holes_found.description: Citation error: 'Cummer, 2000' is cited with DOI 10.1049/ip-map:20030535, but this DOI contains '2003' in its identifier, indicating a 2003 publication, not 2000. The actual Cummer 2000 paper ('Modeling electromagnetic propagation in the earth-ionosphere waveguide') was published in IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation, not the IET journal implied by the 10.1049 DOI prefix. This is either a wrong-year attribution or a wrong-DOI attribution — either way, a dome defender checking citations would flag this as sloppy, undermining our credibility in a section that depends on citing waveguide physics authorities.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify the correct DOI for Cummer's 2000 IEEE TAP paper and update both the year and DOI. If the intended reference is actually a 2003 IET paper, update the citation year to 2003.

holes_found.description: The section says the dome calculates '1.574c' but our own computation shows 40,030/0.08484 = 471,829 km/s = 1.5739c, and Tesla's stated 471,240 km/s = 1.5719c. Neither equals 1.574c. The dome inflates the figure by ~0.13% with no stated justification. Our section adopts the dome's rounded '1.574c' without flagging that this number doesn't follow from the dome's own inputs. This is a missed opportunity: we could point out that the dome can't even compute its own velocity correctly from its own disc diameter and Tesla's measured period.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a brief note: 'The dome claims 1.574c, but 40,030 / 0.08484 = 471,829 km/s = 1.574c only if rounded up; the precise value is 1.5739c. Tesla's own stated 471,240 km/s gives 1.572c. The 0.13% inflation is small but symptomatic of the model's pattern of nudging numbers toward clean-looking values.'

holes_found.description: The section cross-references 'Section 2.9' for the curve-fitting scripts (test_curve_stretching.py, find_curve.py) but does not directly quote or summarize the πR relationship that Section 2.9 establishes. A reader who doesn't click through to Section 2.9 misses the strongest argument for the disc diameter's globe provenance. The link uses onclick='showTab("selftest")' which is correct for the Self-Test tab containing Part 2.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a one-sentence inline summary of 2.9's key finding: 'Section 2.9 shows that the disc radius equals π × R_Earth to four-figure precision — a mathematical fingerprint of spherical arc length that has no explanation on a flat disc.'

holes_found.description: The Tesla patent image and caption are well-integrated and effective — showing Tesla's own spherical Earth diagram is perhaps the single most persuasive piece of evidence in this section. However, the image is served from a local assets/ path ('assets/tesla-patent-787412-full.png'). If this asset is missing or fails to load, the strongest visual evidence disappears. The alt text is adequate but the section's argument depends on the visual.
holes_found.recommendation: No text change needed, but verify the asset exists and is included in the build pipeline. Consider adding a parenthetical in the text: '(the patent's Figure 1 depicts the Earth as a sphere)' so the argument survives even without the image.

stronger_arguments.description: The section could strengthen the circularity argument with a quantitative demonstration. If the dome claims v = disc_diameter / T and we plug in disc_diameter = 2πR_Earth, then v = 2πR / T. But Tesla measured v = circumference / T on the globe, where circumference = 2πR. These are the SAME EQUATION. The dome hasn't discovered anything — it's using v = 2πR/T while claiming v = D/T, and D just happens to equal 2πR. Writing out the algebra makes the circularity undeniable.

The Wait (1966) and waveguide literature could be used more forcefully. The phase velocity formula for the Earth-ionosphere waveguide fundamental mode is v_phase = c / sqrt(1 - (f_c/f)²) where f_c is the cutoff frequency. For ELF at ~8 Hz in a ~80 km cavity, this gives v_phase > c. We could compute the expected superluminal phase velocity from standard waveguide theory and show it matches Tesla's measurement, proving the globe-waveguide explanation is quantitatively complete without invoking any dome parameters.
sources: Budden, K.G. (1961) Radio Waves in the Ionosphere

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model correctly identifies that Tesla measured a superluminal propagation velocity, and correctly notes this is a real physical phenomenon (not a measurement error). Tesla's ~1.57c is genuine ELF/VLF phase velocity in the Earth-ionosphere cavity. The dome also correctly calculates a consistent velocity from its own geometric parameters. The numerical work is competent.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The disc diameter (40,030 km) was fitted to match globe distances (= 2πR_Earth), so using it to compute a velocity that matches a globe-surface measurement is circular. Tesla's own patent shows he understood this as spherical-Earth surface wave propagation. The superluminal velocity is a waveguide effect that requires a spherical cavity geometry to produce — a flat disc with a dome ceiling would produce different waveguide modes and different phase velocities.

text_fingerprint.section_title: The Disc Diameter: 40,030 km and Its Provenance

citations_verified: US Patent 787412 (Tesla, 1905) — confirmed real patent, describes wave propagation on spherical Earth
citations_failed: Cummer, 2000 (DOI 10.1049/ip-map:20030535) — DOI contains '2003' prefix, likely wrong DOI or wrong year. Cummer's 2000 paper is in IEEE TAP, not IET. The DOI and the year cannot both be correct.
citations_unchecked: Wait, 1966 (DOI 10.1029/JZ071i004p01171) — DOI format plausible for JGR 1966. J.R. Wait published extensively on VLF propagation in this era. High confidence but not independently confirmed this run.

deeper_analysis: Section 6.2 is fundamentally sound — the argument that the dome's disc diameter derives from the globe's geometry is correct and well-supported. The Tesla patent with its spherical Earth diagram is an excellent piece of evidence. The waveguide physics is accurately described. However, the section's rhetorical framing has a weakness: it leads with the WEAKER argument (40,030 ≈ 40,075, off by 45 km) rather than the STRONGER argument from Section 2.9 (20,015 = πR_Earth to 114 meters). A dome defender's strongest counterattack is: 'Our diameter is 40,030, not 40,075. If it were the globe's circumference, it would BE 40,075. The 45 km difference proves independent derivation.' This defense fails against the 2πR argument (which has only a 228-meter gap, not a 45 km gap), but Section 6.2 doesn't deploy that argument inline. Additionally, the section misses the opportunity to show the algebraic identity: dome's v = D/T = 2πR/T = circumference/T = globe's v — making the circularity a mathematical tautology rather than a rhetorical claim. The Cummer citation error is a credibility risk in a section that relies on authority citations for waveguide physics. Overall: the argument is correct but the presentation could be tighter, and one citation needs fixing.
